card.title: Fight, Flight, Freeze
card.teaser: Your body makes a split-second decision before your mind even catches up. Here's who's really in charge.
card.coverImagePrompt: A silhouetted human figure standing at a crossroads with three glowing paths — red for fight, blue for flight, white for freeze — surreal, cinematic, dramatic rim lighting against a stormy sky

lessons.title: The car that almost hits you
lessons.body: You step off the curb. Tires screech. Before your brain registers 'car,' you've already jumped back. Heart pounding. Hands shaking. Your body knew before you did — and it's been doing this trick for about 300 million years.

visual.imagePrompt: A pedestrian mid-leap backward as a blurred car rushes past on a rainy street, motion-blur cinematic shot

lessons.title: Meet your amygdala
lessons.body: Deep in your brain sits a pair of almond-shaped clusters called the amygdala. Think of them as your personal smoke detector. They scan for threat 24/7 — and when something pings, they hijack the controls before your rational prefrontal cortex even gets a say. This hijack takes about 12 milliseconds. Conscious thought takes around 500.

keyTerms.definition: Brain region that detects threat and triggers the stress response
The 'thinking' brain that plans and reasons — but reacts slowly

visual.imagePrompt: Cutaway brain illustration with the amygdala glowing red, neural pathways sparking outward

lessons.title: Three ancient doors
lessons.body: Once the alarm fires, your body picks from a very old menu. Fight: adrenaline floods muscles, pupils dilate, you're ready to swing. Flight: same chemistry, but pointed at running. Freeze: the often-forgotten third option — heart rate drops, muscles lock, you go still like a rabbit in tall grass. Which door opens depends on the threat, your history, and sometimes dumb luck.

visual.imagePrompt: Three stylized doorways in a dark forest, each glowing a different color — fists, running legs, frozen statue

lessons.title: Why freezing isn't weakness
lessons.body: For decades, researchers only talked about fight-or-flight. But studies on trauma survivors kept finding something else: people who went completely still, unable to move or speak. Polyvagal theory, developed by neuroscientist Stephen Porges, traces this to the ancient dorsal vagal nerve — a reflex older than mammals. Freezing isn't cowardice. It's a prey animal's last-ditch trick: play dead and hope the predator loses interest.
lessons.funFact: Gazelles that 'freeze' when caught often survive — lions frequently drop limp prey, assuming it's already dead or diseased.

visual.imagePrompt: A deer standing perfectly still in golden meadow grass, eyes wide, perfectly camouflaged

lessons.title: You're not broken — you're ancient
lessons.body: Next time your heart races in a meeting, or you go blank in an argument, remember: that's a 300-million-year-old survival system doing exactly what it evolved to do. Your job isn't to shut it off. It's to notice it, name it, and gently remind your smoke detector that a spreadsheet is not a saber-toothed cat.

quiz.question: Which brain structure triggers the fight-flight-freeze response?
quiz.explanation: The amygdala acts like a threat smoke detector, firing before conscious thought can catch up.

quiz.question: Which response was missing from the original 'fight-or-flight' theory?
quiz.explanation: Freeze was long overlooked — it's tied to the ancient dorsal vagal system and is a prey animal's survival trick.

quiz.question: Roughly how fast does the amygdala react compared to conscious thought?
quiz.explanation: The amygdala can fire in about 12 milliseconds; conscious awareness takes closer to 500.

sources.title: The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-Regulation
sources.publisher: Stephen W. Porges, W. W. Norton & Company

sources.title: The Emotional Brain: The Mysterious Underpinnings of Emotional Life
